SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents a coherent technical formulation (MDP for sense selection, Q-value model (eq.3), and training loss (eq.4)) and derives the doubly-stochastic gradient showing a concrete limitation of a policy-gradient formulation. Experiments (SCWS, synonym selection) and ablation studies are described to support claims. However, some algorithmic details are missing or underspecified in the provided text (the non-parametric sense discovery mechanism is referred to but not fully described) and no statistical significance testing is reported, so minor issues remain that do not fatally affect the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Casting multi-sense selection as an RL problem with a Q-learning reward-passing procedure and joint training of a sense-selection module with a pure sense representation module is a novel combination relative to prior clustering/EM/CRP approaches. The paper also claims a neural non-parametric sense discovery mechanism, which is a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods. The ideas are notable and distinguishable from prior work although they build on established components (CBOW, negative sampling, dropout).

CLARITY.justification: Overall organization follows a standard flow (related work, model, training, experiments) and includes an algorithm listing and derivations. In the provided text there are duplicated passages, some incomplete equations/lines (e.g. softmax replacement line), and limited detail on the non-parametric mechanism, which makes parts hard to follow. Figures and tables are referenced but not shown here, and notation occasionally switches (sigmoid vs softmax), reducing clarity.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates on standard benchmarks (SCWS, several synonym datasets), reports ablation studies removing components (non-parametric, exploration, prior) and provides training/hyperparameter details (Wikipedia dump, window size, negative sampling). Qualitative analyses (sense counts, k-NN, t-SNE) are included. However, some important implementation details of the non-parametric discovery are not fully described in the excerpt and failure modes/limitations are not discussed in depth.

IMPACT.justification: Learning multi-sense embeddings is a significant and active problem in NLP, and introducing an RL-based joint selection/representation approach could influence future research on sense induction and representation learning. The claimed empirical improvements on contextual similarity and practical advantages (linear-time decoding, training on raw corpora) mean the method is likely to be built upon, though it is not clearly transformative across the whole field.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic, methods, and evaluations are squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML conferences: multi-sense word embeddings, comparison to strong baselines, use of large-scale corpora, and novel algorithmic contribution. The paper addresses the right audience and uses evaluation standards expected at these venues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of relevant baselines (clustering, EM, CRP, NP-MSSG, Bartunov et al.), uses standard metrics (MaxSimC/AvgSimC) and notes differences in training data where they exist. Ablation studies are provided to isolate components. Some baselines used different corpora (noted in the text) and the provided excerpt lacks full numeric results and significance analysis, but the comparison strategy appears largely fair and appropriate.
